essay_text:
I was destined to stare at the stars: a few cells into my life, my mother named me Korean for “ .” I’ve
spent countless nights gazing up at the twinkling pinpricks in the black fabric of the sky, flickering at us across
spacetime to remind us how small we are. I’ve grown up reading everything I could find about these gigantic,
hydrogen fireballs and studying the famous thinkers who admired them like Ptolemy, Copernicus, and Sir Isaac
Newton. Still, one astronomer has always stood out among the others: Galileo Galilei.
Galileo was more than a stargazer: his theory of heliocentrism—the idea that the sun is at the center of the
universe—nearly cost him his life. Heliocentrism was considered heretical by the Catholic Church because it
contradicted their teachings about the divine order of the universe: the earth at the center of God’s creation,
humans at the center of the earth, and the church at the center of human society. By placing the sun both literally
and figuratively at the center of his universe, Galileo put science before his faith and freedom and spoke truth to
power.
Reading about this intense, Renaissance conflict about the center of the universe made me reflect on what I put at
the center of mine. We’re often told to place ourselves at the universe’s center, prioritizing our wants and needs
above those of other people. Troubled by this thought, I turned to the stars for answers like I always do. Even
though the sun, is the center of our solar system, I knew it is only one of many sprinkled throughout our
galaxy, a galaxy which is only one of many sprinkled throughout the universe. I wondered what might truly be at
the center of it all. With all the symbolic significance I had assigned to this question, imagine my surprise when I
learned that the universe had no center. Rather, it is constantly expanding in every direction as matter is pulled
together by gravity and pushed apart by dark energy—centerless and eternal, defined by kinetic relationships
instead of static, individual entities.
On Earth, I found my gravity inside the Space Friends, an organization that provides extracurricular activities and
academic support for multicultural and refugee students. I taught math and science classes to elementary school
students and took them on field trips. At Alpaca World, an interactive zoo, I introduced the students to stargazing
in my Star Tent, which I had designed for a tent-making competition and includes a special window so the
occupant can see the sky.
When COVID-19 meant returning to Korea for the spring and summer, I made a five-part video lecture series
about the solar system for Space Friends, which, despite its name, didn’t have astronomy classes. I wanted the
videos to be educational and impactful, so I included humorous introductions where I wore , playing on
my Korean name. When I came to Space Friends for the first time after the lockdown, the children greeted me as
,” clamoring with questions about tidal locking, Mars’ magnetic field and solar wind. Seeing their pure joy
reminded me that my strengths as a scientist are not limited to the lab: they can also be used to shape future
generations of space explorers.
Working with Space Friend’s diverse and overlooked community put my universe’s center outside myself and
taught me the joy of educating others. I’m happy to be one small sun on Earth, warming the lives of people around
me. I have learned that a meaningful life is one that burns passionately and shares that passion with other people,
leaving a positive legacy in a world filled with conflicts and chaos. I strive to be like Galileo: standing up or what is
right, seeking to expand knowledge and putting the good of humanity at the center of our universe, choosing to be
suns over dark energy.
Community Disruption
8
I was planning to build a model of my patented star-gazing tent on Peddie's campus through Club Cosmos this fall.
However, I was unable to do so because of COVID restrictions.
Academically, my first-choice science class for Fall 2020, “XP670: Modern Physics” (Stanford Pre-Collegiate
University-Level Online Math & Physics), was cancelled due to COVID-19. After consulting my school advisor and
science faculty, I opted to take a virtual undergraduate course at Cornell University. Cornell is not offering physics
courses online to pre-college students in Fall 2020, so I took “Exoplanets and Planetary Systems.”
This fall, Peddie also removed some courses from their roster, including my intended, Advanced Seminar in
Computer Science course. So, I opted to take Robotics instead.
Personally, the March closing of school caused me to return to Korea and attend classes virtually from 9pm-4am
local time. I returned to campus for the first part of fall term but was again forced to return to Korea in late October
because of immigration system slowdowns affecting my guardian. I’ve since resumed the same daily schedule.
prompt: Describe a topic, idea, or concept you find so engaging that it makes you lose all track of time. Why does it captivate you? What or who do you turn to when you want to learn more?
persona_prompt: You are a writer who seamlessly integrates scientific concepts, historical narratives, and deeply personal reflection. Your style is characterized by sophisticated vocabulary, often drawing on astronomical metaphors, and a thoughtful, earnest tone. Employ varied sentence structures, moving fluidly between detailed explanations and philosophical insights, always connecting individual experience to broader universal themes. Maintain a voice that is both intellectually curious and genuinely aspirational.
sentence_style: Varied, featuring a mix of longer, complex sentences that elaborate on scientific or philosophical ideas, alongside more concise statements for emphasis. The rhythm is steady and deliberate, with clear transitions that guide the reader through interconnected thoughts. Uses parallelism effectively.
vocabulary_level: Sophisticated — The writer utilizes precise, academic, and often specialized scientific vocabulary (e.g., "heliocentrism," "heretical," "kinetic relationships," "spacetime," "exoplanets") alongside eloquent descriptive language.
structural_patterns: The essay employs a circular or spiraling structure, beginning with a personal hook, expanding to historical and scientific contexts (Galileo, cosmology), then returning to personal application and a future-oriented vision. It uses extended metaphors (the universe, stars, 'center') as a unifying thread, moving from abstract concepts to concrete actions and back to abstract principles.
distinctive_moves: Uses personal origins (name, childhood fascinations) as a foundational metaphor to frame the entire narrative.
Connects grand scientific or historical concepts to deeply personal ethical and philosophical dilemmas.
Employs rich celestial imagery and metaphors to explore themes of identity, purpose, and community engagement.
Synthesizes historical figures' struggles (Galileo) with modern personal challenges and aspirations.
Poses rhetorical questions to guide self-reflection and prompt the reader to consider universal themes.
themes_and_preoccupations: The cosmos, astronomy, and the nature of scientific inquiry.
The individual's place in the universe versus the importance of community.
The pursuit of truth, intellectual courage, and standing up for beliefs.
The power of education, mentorship, and leaving a positive legacy.
self_presentation: The writer presents as introspective, intellectually driven, deeply empathetic, and resilient. They are an aspiring scientist and educator, characterized by a humble confidence in their ability to make a meaningful impact, positioning themselves as a thoughtful leader and a 'small sun on Earth'.
cultural_or_contextual_markers: Korean heritage (name, experiences returning to Korea), involvement with multicultural and refugee communities (Space Friends), resilience in adapting to global disruptions (COVID-19, immigration challenges), and engagement with advanced academic subjects (physics, astronomy) at a high level.
avoid_in_imitation: Overly casual or colloquial language that would undermine the intellectual tone.
A lack of deep personal reflection or connection between disparate ideas.
Simple, declarative sentences without the nuanced explanation or metaphorical depth.
Any tone that feels self-aggrandizing rather than earnestly aspirational.
sample_sentence: Much like the gravitational pull that shapes distant nebulae, our deepest convictions can draw us toward unexpected orbits of service, illuminating new constellations of purpose within our ever-expanding lives.
